title: Neonatal HYPERBILIRUBINEMIA and PHOTOTHERAPY in a 36-Hour-Old Late-Preterm (36+0 Weeks) Exclusively Breastfed Newborn Boy with ABO Set-Up (Mother O+, Baby A+), Weight Loss 9% from Birth, and a Total Serum Bilirubin in the AAP HIGH-INTERMEDIATE Risk Zone: Distinguishing PHYSIOLOGIC vs PATHOLOGIC (Jaundice <24 Hours = Pathologic) vs BREASTFEEDING vs BREAST-MILK Jaundice, Plotting Bilirubin on the Bhutani Nomogram by HOUR OF LIFE, Safe Phototherapy Care (EYE Shields, Maximum Skin Exposure, Reposition q2-3h, Temperature, HYDRATION via Increased BREASTFEEDING — NOT Water/Dextrose), Recognizing ACUTE BILIRUBIN ENCEPHALOPATHY, and Parent Teaching for Discharge
subtitle: Neonatal jaundice — clinically visible yellow discoloration of skin and sclerae when the total serum bilirubin (TSB) exceeds about 5 mg/dL — is one of the most common newborn conditions but also one of the most under-recognized as DANGEROUS when it occurs at the wrong time. UNCONJUGATED (indirect) bilirubin from red-cell breakdown is bound to albumin in plasma; when albumin-binding is exceeded the LIPOPHILIC free fraction crosses the blood-brain barrier and stains/injures the basal ganglia/brainstem nuclei — producing ACUTE BILIRUBIN ENCEPHALOPATHY (BIND) and, if not reversed, the chronic syndrome of KERNICTERUS (athetoid cerebral palsy, sensorineural hearing loss, oculomotor paralysis, dental enamel dysplasia). The most important nursing distinction is PHYSIOLOGIC vs PATHOLOGIC. PHYSIOLOGIC jaundice appears AFTER 24 hours of life, peaks day 3-5 in term infants and day 5-7 in preterm infants, and resolves by 1-2 weeks (term) or 2-4 weeks (preterm); it reflects high neonatal RBC mass, short RBC lifespan, immature hepatic conjugation, and active enterohepatic recirculation. PATHOLOGIC jaundice is signaled by ANY of: jaundice within the FIRST 24 HOURS OF LIFE (always pathologic), rise > 0.2 mg/dL/hour or > 5 mg/dL/day, TSB exceeding age-and-gestation thresholds, persistence beyond 2 weeks (term) or 3 weeks (preterm), or a CONJUGATED (direct) fraction > 1 mg/dL or > 20% of total (think biliary atresia, sepsis). Hemolytic causes (ABO/Rh incompatibility, G6PD deficiency, hereditary spherocytosis) produce early and severe rises. BREASTFEEDING JAUNDICE (early, first week) is essentially a feeding problem — suboptimal breastfeeding → caloric/volume insufficiency → dehydration and slowed bilirubin elimination via enterohepatic recirculation; the treatment is to OPTIMIZE BREASTFEEDING (frequency, latch, lactation support) — NOT to stop breastfeeding and NOT to give water or dextrose-water (these worsen jaundice and risk hyponatremia). BREAST-MILK JAUNDICE (later onset, days 7-10, can persist weeks) is thought to involve breast-milk components that increase enterohepatic recirculation; breastfeeding is almost never interrupted; phototherapy is used if needed. RISK ASSESSMENT uses HOURS OF LIFE (not just days), plotting TSB or transcutaneous bilirubin (TcB) on the BHUTANI NOMOGRAM by hour of life to place the infant in a LOW / LOW-INTERMEDIATE / HIGH-INTERMEDIATE / HIGH-risk zone; the 2022 AAP guidelines provide specific phototherapy and exchange-transfusion thresholds by gestational age, hours of life, and hyperbilirubinemia neurotoxicity risk factors (G6PD deficiency, hemolysis, sepsis, hypoalbuminemia, asphyxia/instability, gestation < 38 weeks). Risk factors include LATE-PRETERM birth (35-36 weeks — much higher risk than term), ABO/Rh set-up, East Asian ancestry, family history of significant jaundice, cephalohematoma/bruising, exclusive breastfeeding with > 10% weight loss, G6PD deficiency, and jaundice within the first 24 hours. PHOTOTHERAPY uses BLUE-GREEN visible light (peak ~ 460 nm) that PHOTOISOMERIZES unconjugated bilirubin in the skin into water-soluble lumirubin and configurational isomers that are excreted in bile and urine WITHOUT conjugation. Nursing care principles: PROTECT THE EYES with opaque eye shields throughout phototherapy (prevent retinal damage) — remove only briefly during feeds for parent contact; MAXIMIZE SKIN EXPOSURE — diaper only (or naked in a controlled thermal environment) — and REPOSITION every 2-3 hours to expose all skin surfaces; use a fiber-optic 'bili blanket' under or against the baby per device; monitor TEMPERATURE closely (risk of either hyperthermia or hypothermia depending on system); ensure HYDRATION via INCREASED BREASTFEEDING (about every 2-3 hours, 8-12 feedings/24 hours) with lactation support — DO NOT give plain water or dextrose-water (worsens jaundice via decreased bile flow and risks hyponatremia); monitor I/O, daily weight, wet diapers, and stool counts (stools help excrete bilirubin); expect loose green stools and a mild transient rash; AVOID lotions/oils on the skin (can burn); DO NOT cover with blankets; check TSB on the schedule the provider orders (often every 4-12 hours depending on level/risk) and discontinue phototherapy when below the AAP threshold, then re-check 'rebound' bilirubin 12-24 hours after stopping. IMPORTANT: phototherapy is for UNCONJUGATED hyperbilirubinemia; do NOT use phototherapy in conjugated/direct hyperbilirubinemia — risk of 'BRONZE BABY' syndrome (gray-brown skin discoloration from porphyrin-photoproduct accumulation). EXCHANGE TRANSFUSION (double-volume) is reserved for TSB above the exchange threshold or for signs of ACUTE BILIRUBIN ENCEPHALOPATHY: early — lethargy, poor feeding, hypotonia, high-pitched cry; intermediate — irritability, hypertonia/retrocollis/opisthotonus, fever; late — coma, seizures, death. PARENT TEACHING for safe discharge: recognize cephalocaudal jaundice progression (face → chest → abdomen → legs → soles); feed adequately (8-12 feedings/24 hours, count wet/stool diapers); attend ALL bilirubin follow-up appointments (especially within the first 3-5 days); warning signs to call for or return for evaluation — increasing jaundice extending to the legs/soles, lethargy or poor feeding, fewer than 6 wet diapers/day after day 4, high-pitched cry, irritability, fever, or jaundice persisting beyond 2 weeks. PERTINENT MISTAKES TO AVOID: giving water or dextrose-water to a jaundiced breastfed newborn; routinely interrupting breastfeeding for jaundice; covering with blankets or using lotions during phototherapy; turning off the lights for prolonged periods for feedings (only brief, structured removal); leaving the eye shields off; using phototherapy for conjugated hyperbilirubinemia; relying on visual estimation alone above the chest (especially in darker skin tones) — always confirm with TcB or TSB. NURSING ROLE: recognize and risk-stratify neonatal jaundice using hours of life and the Bhutani nomogram with AAP thresholds; deliver safe phototherapy (eyes protected, skin exposed and repositioned, temperature stable, hydration via breastfeeding + lactation support, no water/dextrose); monitor bilirubin trends and watch for ACUTE BILIRUBIN ENCEPHALOPATHY; promote and support breastfeeding throughout; teach parents the cephalocaudal progression, feeding goals, warning signs, and follow-up plan; and coordinate discharge with timely outpatient follow-up.

patient.name: Baby Boy A. Vargas
patient.code: Full code; well newborn now under inpatient phototherapy for hyperbilirubinemia; goal = safe phototherapy nursing care (eyes protected, skin exposed and repositioned, temperature stable), increased BREASTFEEDING for hydration (no water/dextrose), bilirubin trend monitoring, recognition of acute bilirubin encephalopathy, and parent education for discharge and follow-up
patient.admitDate: Late-preterm boy born at 36+0 weeks by SVD to a 28-year-old G1P1 mother (blood type O+) with an uncomplicated pregnancy. Birth weight 3.4 kg; cord blood type A+, Coombs (DAT) weakly positive (consistent with ABO set-up). Exclusively breastfed; weight loss is 9% at 36 hours of life (high-end physiologic limit); breastfeeding is suboptimal (intermittent latch, fewer than 8 feeds/24 hours). Visible jaundice on the face and chest at 30 hours. Transcutaneous bilirubin elevated → TSB drawn and plotted by hour of life on the Bhutani nomogram → HIGH-INTERMEDIATE risk zone for a 36-weeker with ABO set-up and weight loss; the 2022 AAP threshold for phototherapy at 36 hours for a 36-week infant with neurotoxicity risk is exceeded → conventional intensive phototherapy initiated. Mother is anxious and wants to keep breastfeeding.
patient.room: Postpartum/newborn unit; bassinet under overhead phototherapy lights with fiber-optic blanket as adjunct; eye shields in place; isolette available for temperature support; interdisciplinary team (pediatrics, neonatal RN, lactation consultant, social work, primary care for follow-up)

vitals.bp: Not routinely checked in well newborn
vitals.temp: 36.8°C (axillary); visible jaundice on face and chest (cephalocaudal progression); active, slightly less vigorous than expected; weight loss 9% from birth; latch intermittent → high suspicion for hyperbilirubinemia in a late-preterm ABO-set-up exclusively breastfed newborn

vitals.time: 36 hours of life — TSB plotted, phototherapy started
vitals.temp: 36.9°C (axillary); TSB plotted by HOUR OF LIFE on Bhutani nomogram → HIGH-INTERMEDIATE risk zone; 2022 AAP phototherapy threshold exceeded for a 36-week infant with neurotoxicity risk factors (late-preterm, ABO set-up with Coombs+) → intensive phototherapy initiated; lactation consult triggered

37.0°C (axillary, stable); opaque EYE SHIELDS in place + verified intact; diaper only, MAXIMUM SKIN EXPOSURE; REPOSITION q2-3 hours; bilirubin lights at correct distance; fiber-optic blanket adjunct; thermal environment monitored; BREASTFEEDING q2-3h (8-12/24h) with lactation support — NO water/dextrose-water

37.0°C; TSB trending DOWN, infant feeding more vigorously with lactation support, more wet diapers and loose green stools (expected bilirubin excretion), jaundice receding cephalocaudally; weight stabilizing; no signs of acute bilirubin encephalopathy (no lethargy/hypotonia/high-pitched cry)

vitals.time: TSB below threshold — phototherapy stopped + parent teaching
vitals.temp: 37.0°C; TSB below AAP threshold → phototherapy discontinued; eye shields removed; rebound TSB check ordered 12-24 hours after stopping; parents taught cephalocaudal jaundice check, 8-12 feeds/24h with wet/stool diaper counts, warning signs (lethargy, poor feeding, fewer wet diapers, high-pitched cry, jaundice extending to legs/soles, >2 weeks duration); outpatient bilirubin follow-up scheduled 24-48 hours after discharge

vitals.time: Outpatient follow-up — rebound stable, breastfeeding established
vitals.temp: 37.0°C; rebound TSB within safe range for hour of life; weight gain resumed (back to birth weight by ~ day 10); BREASTFEEDING established with lactation follow-up; no neurologic concerns; mother confident; pediatric medical home follow-up confirmed

labs.n: PRESENTATION + risk-stratifying neonatal jaundice (physiologic vs pathologic; the Bhutani nomogram by HOUR OF LIFE)
labs.v: Late-preterm (36+0 weeks) exclusively breastfed boy, 36 hours of life, mother O+, baby A+, Coombs (DAT) weakly positive (ABO set-up), birth weight 3.4 kg, weight loss 9% from birth, suboptimal breastfeeding (intermittent latch, < 8 feeds/24 hours), visible jaundice on face and chest at 30 hours. Distinguish PHYSIOLOGIC (after 24 hours, peaks day 3-5 term/day 5-7 preterm, resolves 1-2 weeks term/2-4 weeks preterm) vs PATHOLOGIC (jaundice within the first 24 hours of life, rise > 0.2 mg/dL/hour or > 5 mg/dL/day, exceeds age/gestation thresholds, persists > 2-3 weeks, or a conjugated/direct fraction > 1 mg/dL or > 20% of total). Hemolytic causes (ABO/Rh, G6PD, spherocytosis) produce early and severe rises. BREASTFEEDING JAUNDICE (early, first week) reflects suboptimal feeding/dehydration → enterohepatic recirculation — treat with OPTIMIZED breastfeeding (frequency, latch, lactation support); BREAST-MILK JAUNDICE (later, days 7-10, can persist weeks) — breastfeeding is almost never interrupted. RISK ASSESSMENT uses HOURS OF LIFE on the BHUTANI NOMOGRAM (low / low-intermediate / high-intermediate / high zone) with the 2022 AAP gestation/hour/risk-stratified phototherapy and exchange thresholds. RISK FACTORS in this baby: LATE-PRETERM 36+0, ABO set-up with Coombs+, exclusive breastfeeding with 9% weight loss. Today's plan: intensive phototherapy with safe nursing care, lactation support, bilirubin trend monitoring, vigilance for acute bilirubin encephalopathy, and parent teaching for safe discharge and follow-up.
labs.r: Jaundice = TSB > 5 mg/dL clinically visible. PHYSIOLOGIC (after 24h, peaks d3-5 term/d5-7 preterm, resolves 1-2 wk/2-4 wk) vs PATHOLOGIC (< 24h ever; rise > 0.2/h or > 5/d; over threshold; persists > 2-3 wk; conjugated > 1 or > 20% — think biliary atresia/sepsis). BREASTFEEDING jaundice (early, feeding problem — optimize, don't stop) vs BREAST-MILK jaundice (later, persists; rarely interrupt feeding). Risk assess on Bhutani by HOUR OF LIFE + AAP thresholds by gestation + risk factors (late-preterm, ABO/Rh, Coombs+, G6PD, hemolysis, sepsis, hypoalbuminemia). RN role: risk-stratify and act on hour of life.

labs.n: Phototherapy mechanism + safe nursing care (EYES + SKIN + TEMPERATURE + HYDRATION)
labs.v: Phototherapy uses BLUE-GREEN visible light (peak ~ 460 nm) that PHOTOISOMERIZES unconjugated bilirubin in the skin into water-soluble lumirubin and configurational isomers excreted in bile and urine WITHOUT hepatic conjugation. Nursing care: PROTECT THE EYES with opaque eye shields throughout phototherapy (prevent retinal damage); remove only briefly and intentionally during structured parent-feeding interaction. MAXIMIZE SKIN EXPOSURE — diaper only (or naked in a controlled thermal environment) — and REPOSITION every 2-3 hours to expose all skin surfaces; a fiber-optic 'bili blanket' may be used in direct contact and combined with overhead lights for intensive phototherapy. Maintain correct LAMP DISTANCE per manufacturer. Monitor TEMPERATURE every 1-2 hours (risk of hyperthermia under lights OR hypothermia if uncovered in a cool room — an isolette stabilizes thermal balance). Ensure HYDRATION through INCREASED BREASTFEEDING (about every 2-3 hours, 8-12 feedings/24 hours) with lactation support; DO NOT give plain water or dextrose-water (worsens jaundice via decreased bile flow and risks hyponatremia); supplement only with expressed breast milk or formula per provider if intake is inadequate. Monitor I/O, DAILY WEIGHT, WET DIAPERS, and STOOL COUNTS (stools help excrete bilirubin); expect loose green stools and a mild transient rash. AVOID lotions, oils, or sunscreen on the skin (can burn under light); DO NOT cover with blankets. Check TSB on the provider-ordered schedule (often every 4-12 hours), discontinue phototherapy when below the AAP threshold, and obtain a REBOUND TSB 12-24 hours after stopping. PHOTOTHERAPY is for UNCONJUGATED hyperbilirubinemia — DO NOT use it in CONJUGATED (direct) hyperbilirubinemia (risk of 'BRONZE BABY' syndrome — gray-brown skin discoloration).
labs.r: Mechanism = blue-green light (~460 nm) PHOTOISOMERIZES skin unconjugated bilirubin → water-soluble forms excreted in bile/urine WITHOUT conjugation. Care: OPAQUE EYE SHIELDS on throughout (brief removal for parent feeds); MAX SKIN exposure (diaper only) + REPOSITION q2-3h; fiber-optic blanket adjunct; correct lamp distance; TEMPERATURE q1-2h (risk hyperthermia under lights or hypothermia); HYDRATION via INCREASED breastfeeding q2-3h with lactation support (NO water/dextrose); monitor I/O + daily weight + wet/stool diapers (loose green stools expected); NO lotions/oils/blankets; TSB q4-12h per order; stop below AAP threshold + REBOUND TSB 12-24h after stop. UNCONJUGATED only — NOT for conjugated (bronze baby).

labs.n: ACUTE BILIRUBIN ENCEPHALOPATHY (BIND) + kernicterus + exchange transfusion + breastfeeding management
labs.v: Unconjugated bilirubin is lipophilic; when albumin binding is exceeded, free bilirubin crosses the blood-brain barrier and injures the basal ganglia and brainstem nuclei. ACUTE BILIRUBIN ENCEPHALOPATHY (BIND) progresses in three phases: EARLY — lethargy, poor feeding, hypotonia, high-pitched cry; INTERMEDIATE — irritability, hypertonia/retrocollis/opisthotonus (arched back), and fever; LATE — coma, seizures, and death. Untreated, this leads to CHRONIC BILIRUBIN ENCEPHALOPATHY (KERNICTERUS) — athetoid CEREBRAL PALSY, sensorineural HEARING LOSS, oculomotor paralysis (upward-gaze palsy), and dental enamel dysplasia. EXCHANGE TRANSFUSION (double-volume) is reserved for TSB above the AAP exchange threshold or for signs of acute bilirubin encephalopathy: it replaces about 85% of the infant's blood with donor blood, removing bilirubin and any antibody-coated red cells while restoring intravascular volume; it is performed in a high-acuity setting with continuous monitoring. BREASTFEEDING MANAGEMENT during phototherapy: continue and OPTIMIZE breastfeeding with lactation support; routine interruption of breastfeeding is NOT recommended; if intake is inadequate, supplement with expressed breast milk or formula per provider — never give water or dextrose-water (worsens jaundice and risks hyponatremia). For severe ABO/Rh hemolytic disease, IVIG may reduce hemolysis and the need for exchange transfusion. In G6PD-deficient infants, avoid oxidant medications and exposures.
labs.r: Free unconjugated bilirubin crosses BBB → basal ganglia/brainstem injury. BIND phases: EARLY (lethargy/poor feeding/hypotonia/high-pitched cry) → INTERMEDIATE (irritability/hypertonia/retrocollis-opisthotonus/fever) → LATE (coma/seizures/death). Untreated → KERNICTERUS (athetoid CP, SNHL, upward-gaze palsy, dental enamel dysplasia). EXCHANGE TRANSFUSION (double-volume) for TSB above AAP exchange threshold or BIND signs — replaces ~85% blood. Continue + OPTIMIZE breastfeeding (lactation support); don't routinely interrupt; supplement w/ expressed milk/formula if needed; NEVER water/dextrose-water. IVIG for severe ABO/Rh hemolysis. RN role: recognize BIND + protect feeding.

labs.n: Parent teaching + follow-up + Pertinent NEGATIVES + protective factors
labs.v: PARENT TEACHING for safe discharge: recognize the CEPHALOCAUDAL progression of jaundice (face → chest → abdomen → thighs → legs and soles — increasing extent = increasing severity); FEEDING goals (8-12 feedings/24 hours; count WET DIAPERS — about 6+/day after day 4 — and STOOL diapers; track latch and time at breast); attend ALL outpatient bilirubin follow-up appointments (especially within the first 3-5 days for late-preterm/risk-factor infants); WARNING SIGNS that require evaluation or a call to the provider — jaundice extending to the LEGS or SOLES, lethargy or poor feeding, fewer than 6 wet diapers/day after day 4, HIGH-PITCHED CRY, irritability, arched back/abnormal tone, fever, or jaundice persisting beyond 2 weeks (term) or 3 weeks (preterm). Reinforce that breastfeeding is supported and rarely interrupted, and that water or dextrose-water are NOT given to a jaundiced newborn. HOME PHOTOTHERAPY (fiber-optic blanket) is appropriate for selected infants once below the inpatient threshold; the same care principles apply (eye protection per device, maximum skin exposure, hydration via feeding, monitoring). FOLLOW-UP: rebound TSB 12-24 hours after stopping phototherapy; primary care follow-up within 24-48 hours of discharge; lactation follow-up; weight check. PERTINENT NEGATIVES (correct practice): the nurse did NOT give water or dextrose-water to the jaundiced breastfed newborn; did NOT routinely stop breastfeeding for jaundice; did NOT cover the infant with blankets or use lotions during phototherapy; did NOT leave eye shields off the infant under the lights; did NOT use phototherapy on a conjugated hyperbilirubinemia; did NOT rely on visual estimation alone (always confirmed with TcB or TSB); and did NOT skip the rebound TSB or outpatient follow-up appointment. PROTECTIVE FACTORS: timely risk stratification by HOUR OF LIFE on the Bhutani nomogram with AAP thresholds; safe phototherapy (eyes protected, skin exposed and repositioned, temperature stable, correct distance, no lotions/blankets); HYDRATION via increased breastfeeding with lactation support; bilirubin trend monitoring and a rebound TSB; vigilance for acute bilirubin encephalopathy; thorough parent teaching with TEACH-BACK; coordinated outpatient follow-up; and a stigma-free, supportive partnership with the mother. EXPECTED outcome: bilirubin trends down with intensive phototherapy and improved feeding, no acute bilirubin encephalopathy, breastfeeding established, weight back to birth by ~ day 10, and a safe transition to outpatient follow-up.
labs.r: Parent teaching: cephalocaudal jaundice check + 8-12 feeds/24h w/ wet/stool diaper counts + ALL outpatient bili follow-ups + warning signs (jaundice to legs/soles, lethargy, poor feeding, <6 wet diapers/day after d4, high-pitched cry, arched back/tone, fever, >2-wk duration). Breastfeeding continued; NO water/dextrose. Home phototherapy w/ same rules if selected. Rebound TSB 12-24h after stop; PCP follow-up 24-48h. Negatives confirm correct practice (no water/dextrose, no routine BF stop, no blankets/lotions, no eye shields off, not on conjugated bili, no visual-only, no skipping rebound/follow-up). Protective: timely risk strat + safe phototherapy + hydration via BF + bili trend + BIND vigilance + parent teach-back + outpatient follow-up + partnership.

labs.n: Total serum bilirubin (TSB) at 36 hours of life
labs.v: 13.2 mg/dL (high-intermediate Bhutani zone for 36 hours of life; AAP phototherapy threshold for a 36-week infant with neurotoxicity risk factors EXCEEDED — phototherapy initiated)
labs.r: Threshold depends on hour of life, gestation, and risk factors (use AAP nomogram)

labs.v: 0.4 mg/dL (well within normal; UNCONJUGATED hyperbilirubinemia — appropriate for phototherapy; NOT bronze baby risk)
Weakly positive (consistent with ABO set-up — mother O+, baby A+; supports ongoing low-grade hemolysis as a neurotoxicity risk factor)
16.2 g/dL (within newborn normal range; no significant anemia from hemolysis at this time — trend with serial values)

nursesNote: Postpartum/newborn unit — late-preterm 36+0 boy, DOL 1.5 (36 hours of life), birth wt 3.4 kg, weight loss 9% from birth, exclusively breastfed (suboptimal latch, < 8 feeds/24h), mother O+ / baby A+ (ABO SET-UP) with Coombs (DAT) weakly positive. Visible jaundice face + chest at 30h → TcB → TSB drawn and plotted by HOUR OF LIFE on Bhutani nomogram → HIGH-INTERMEDIATE risk zone; 2022 AAP phototherapy threshold EXCEEDED for a 36-week infant w/ neurotoxicity risk factors (late-preterm + ABO set-up + Coombs+ + weight loss); intensive phototherapy initiated. Distinguish physiologic (after 24h, peaks d3-5 term/d5-7 preterm) vs pathologic (< 24h, rise > 0.2/h or > 5/d, over threshold, persists > 2-3 wk, conjugated > 1 or > 20%). His pattern = combined breastfeeding-jaundice (poor intake) + low-grade ABO hemolysis in a late-preterm — risk stratified by HOUR OF LIFE. PHOTOTHERAPY CARE: opaque EYE SHIELDS on throughout (off briefly only for structured parent-feeds); diaper only — MAXIMUM SKIN EXPOSURE; REPOSITION q2-3h; correct lamp distance + fiber-optic blanket adjunct; temperature monitored q1-2h (stable 36.9-37.0°C; isolette ready); HYDRATION via INCREASED BREASTFEEDING q2-3h (8-12 feeds/24h) w/ lactation consult — NO water/dextrose-water (worsen jaundice + hyponatremia risk); supplement w/ expressed milk/formula per provider only if intake inadequate; monitor I/O + daily weight + wet/stool diapers (loose green stools expected); NO lotions/oils/blankets; TSB on provider schedule; UNCONJUGATED only — phototherapy NOT for conjugated (bronze baby). VIGILANCE for BIND/acute bilirubin encephalopathy: early (lethargy/poor feeding/hypotonia/high-pitched cry) → intermediate (irritability/hypertonia/retrocollis-opisthotonus/fever) → late (coma/seizures/death) → KERNICTERUS (athetoid CP + SNHL + upward-gaze palsy + dental enamel dysplasia). Exchange transfusion reserved for TSB > AAP exchange threshold or BIND signs (double-volume, ~85% blood replaced). IVIG considered for severe ABO/Rh hemolysis. After 12h phototherapy + lactation support: TSB trending DOWN, infant feeding more vigorously, more wet diapers + loose green stools, jaundice receding cephalocaudally, no BIND signs → TSB below AAP threshold → phototherapy STOPPED; eye shields removed; REBOUND TSB ordered 12-24h after stop. PARENT TEACHING + DISCHARGE: cephalocaudal jaundice check (face → trunk → legs/soles = worse), 8-12 feeds/24h w/ wet/stool diaper counts (6+/day after d4), attend ALL outpatient bili follow-ups (24-48h post-discharge for this risk profile), warning signs → call/return (jaundice to legs/soles, lethargy, poor feeding, fewer wet diapers, HIGH-PITCHED CRY, arched back/tone, fever, > 2 wk duration); breastfeeding continued and supported. NEGATIVES (correct practice): did NOT give water/dextrose-water, did NOT routinely stop breastfeeding, did NOT cover w/ blankets or use lotions, did NOT leave eye shields off, did NOT phototherapy a conjugated bili, did NOT visual-estimate alone above the chest (always TcB/TSB), did NOT skip rebound TSB or follow-up. Outpatient follow-up: rebound TSB safe; weight gain resumed (back to birth wt ~ day 10); breastfeeding established; no neuro concerns; pediatric medical home follow-up confirmed.
providerOrders: DIAGNOSIS: neonatal indirect (unconjugated) hyperbilirubinemia in a 36+0-week late-preterm exclusively breastfed boy with ABO set-up (mother O+, baby A+) and weight loss 9% from birth; TSB in the high-intermediate Bhutani zone at 36 hours of life — AAP phototherapy threshold exceeded with neurotoxicity risk factors

PHOTOTHERAPY: initiate intensive conventional phototherapy (overhead lights + fiber-optic blanket); ensure correct lamp distance per device; for UNCONJUGATED hyperbilirubinemia only — do NOT use phototherapy in conjugated (direct) hyperbilirubinemia
EYE PROTECTION: opaque eye shields in place throughout phototherapy; remove only briefly and intentionally during structured parent-feeding interaction

SKIN EXPOSURE: diaper only (or naked in a controlled thermal environment); reposition every 2-3 hours to expose all skin surfaces; no lotions, oils, sunscreen, or blankets on the infant under the lights
TEMPERATURE: monitor axillary temperature every 1-2 hours; use an isolette/radiant warmer as needed to maintain thermal balance

HYDRATION/FEEDING: breastfeed every 2-3 hours (target 8-12 feedings per 24 hours) with lactation support; DO NOT give plain water or dextrose-water; supplement with expressed breast milk or formula per provider only if intake remains inadequate; track wet and stool diaper counts and daily weights

BILIRUBIN MONITORING: total serum bilirubin every 4-12 hours per provider order; plot by hour of life on the Bhutani nomogram; discontinue phototherapy when TSB is below the AAP threshold; check a REBOUND TSB 12-24 hours after stopping phototherapy

ENCEPHALOPATHY SURVEILLANCE: assess and document tone, feeding, cry, alertness, and reflexes each shift; immediately escalate for signs of acute bilirubin encephalopathy (lethargy, poor feeding, hypotonia, high-pitched cry → irritability, hypertonia/retrocollis-opisthotonus, fever → coma, seizures); prepare for exchange transfusion if TSB above the AAP exchange threshold or BIND signs are present; consider IVIG for severe ABO/Rh hemolysis

PARENT EDUCATION: cephalocaudal jaundice progression, 8-12 feedings/24 hours with diaper counts, no water/dextrose-water, warning signs (jaundice to legs/soles, lethargy, poor feeding, fewer than 6 wet diapers/day after day 4, high-pitched cry, irritability, abnormal tone, fever, jaundice > 2 weeks); reinforce supported continued breastfeeding

DISCHARGE/FOLLOW-UP: outpatient bilirubin recheck within 24-48 hours of discharge; primary care follow-up; lactation follow-up; consider home phototherapy via fiber-optic blanket for eligible infants below the inpatient threshold (same eye/skin/feeding/monitoring rules apply)

steps.title: Recognize Cues
steps.sub: What matters about this jaundiced late-preterm newborn?
steps.inst: Select ALL findings/considerations that are clinically significant.

opts.text: Late-preterm (36+0), exclusively breastfed with suboptimal latch and 9% weight loss, ABO set-up with a weakly positive Coombs — multiple neurotoxicity risk factors raise the AAP phototherapy threshold concern
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — late-preterm + ABO set-up + weight loss + Coombs+ make him a high-risk infant. RN role: identify risk factors that lower the action threshold.

opts.text: Jaundice appeared after 24 hours of life (consistent with non-pathologic timing) but the TSB plotted by HOUR OF LIFE on the Bhutani nomogram is in the high-intermediate zone above the AAP phototherapy threshold
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — hour-of-life risk stratification drives the decision; jaundice within the first 24 hours would itself be pathologic. RN role: risk-stratify by hour of life.

opts.text: Phototherapy with blue-green light photoisomerizes unconjugated bilirubin in the skin into water-soluble products excreted in bile/urine without hepatic conjugation
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — understanding the mechanism guides safe nursing care. RN role: deliver effective phototherapy.

opts.text: Safe phototherapy nursing care: opaque eye shields throughout, maximum skin exposure (diaper only) with q2-3h repositioning, correct lamp distance, temperature monitoring, no lotions/blankets
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — these are the core safety steps. RN role: eyes + skin + temperature + technique.

opts.text: Hydration is provided through INCREASED BREASTFEEDING with lactation support (about every 2-3 hours, 8-12 feedings/24 hours); plain water or dextrose-water are NOT given (worsen jaundice and risk hyponatremia)
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — the breastfeeding-jaundice rule. RN role: optimize feeding, never water/dextrose.

opts.text: Watch for acute bilirubin encephalopathy (lethargy/poor feeding/hypotonia/high-pitched cry → irritability/hypertonia/retrocollis-opisthotonus/fever → coma/seizures) and the chronic syndrome KERNICTERUS
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — the catastrophe phototherapy is designed to prevent. RN role: BIND vigilance.

opts.text: Give plain water and dextrose-water between feedings to help flush out the bilirubin
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. Water/dextrose-water worsen jaundice (decreased caloric intake and bile flow) and cause hyponatremia. Treat with optimized breastfeeding.

opts.text: Stop breastfeeding routinely whenever a newborn becomes jaundiced
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. Routine interruption of breastfeeding is not recommended; optimize breastfeeding with lactation support and supplement with expressed milk/formula per provider if intake is inadequate.

opts.text: Jaundice within the first 24 hours of life is ALWAYS pathologic; physiologic jaundice appears after 24 hours and peaks day 3-5 (term) or day 5-7 (preterm)
opts.rat: CORRECT — the timing rule. Foundation for recognizing pathologic jaundice.

opts.text: Bilirubin is plotted by HOUR OF LIFE on the Bhutani nomogram; the 2022 AAP guidelines provide gestation- and risk-adjusted phototherapy and exchange thresholds
opts.rat: CORRECT — risk-stratification principle. Foundation for the decision to treat.

opts.text: Phototherapy uses blue-green light to photoisomerize unconjugated bilirubin into water-soluble forms excreted without conjugation; it is for UNCONJUGATED hyperbilirubinemia only
opts.rat: CORRECT — mechanism and indication. Foundation for safe use.

opts.text: Phototherapy used in CONJUGATED (direct) hyperbilirubinemia risks 'bronze baby' syndrome and is not appropriate
opts.rat: CORRECT — contraindication. Foundation for correct selection.

opts.text: Hydration during phototherapy is through INCREASED BREASTFEEDING with lactation support; water and dextrose-water are NOT given
opts.rat: CORRECT — hydration principle. Foundation for correcting breastfeeding jaundice safely.

opts.text: Acute bilirubin encephalopathy presents with lethargy, poor feeding, hypotonia, and a high-pitched cry, then irritability, hypertonia/retrocollis-opisthotonus, and fever; untreated kernicterus = athetoid CP, sensorineural hearing loss, oculomotor paralysis, dental enamel dysplasia
opts.rat: CORRECT — BIND/kernicterus principle. Foundation for vigilance.

opts.text: Visual estimation alone is reliable for determining bilirubin level, especially in darker-skinned infants, and TSB/TcB are not necessary
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. Visual estimation underestimates jaundice (especially in darker skin tones); always confirm with transcutaneous bilirubin or serum bilirubin.

opts.text: Phototherapy is best delivered with the infant fully wrapped in blankets and lotion-treated skin to keep them warm and moisturized
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. Blankets block the light, and lotions/oils can burn under phototherapy. Maximize skin exposure with diaper only and avoid skin products.

opts.text: Risk-stratify by HOUR OF LIFE on the Bhutani nomogram with AAP thresholds (gestation + neurotoxicity risk factors) and initiate phototherapy if indicated
opts.rat: #1 — HIGHEST. The right action depends on the right risk classification. RN role: risk-stratify, then act.

opts.text: Deliver safe phototherapy — opaque eye shields throughout, maximum skin exposure with q2-3h repositioning, correct lamp distance, temperature monitoring, no lotions/blankets
opts.rat: #2 — HIGH. Safe technique drives effectiveness and prevents harm. RN role: technique.

opts.text: Maintain hydration through INCREASED breastfeeding with lactation support (about every 2-3 hours, 8-12 feedings/24 hours); no water or dextrose-water
opts.rat: #3 — Hydration via feeding is essential to clear bilirubin via stool/urine. RN role: feeding + lactation.

opts.text: Monitor TSB trend on schedule, stop phototherapy when below the AAP threshold, and check a REBOUND TSB 12-24 hours later; surveil for acute bilirubin encephalopathy throughout
opts.rat: #4 — Trend monitoring + BIND vigilance close the safety loop. RN role: monitor + vigilance.

opts.text: Teach the parents (cephalocaudal check, 8-12 feeds/24h, warning signs, no water/dextrose) and coordinate outpatient follow-up (rebound TSB, primary care, lactation)
opts.rat: #5 — Closes the loop into the home and prevents readmission. RN role: teaching + follow-up.

steps.title: Generate Solutions
steps.inst: For EACH action, classify as INDICATED or NOT INDICATED for this jaundiced newborn.

opts.text: Plot the TSB by HOUR OF LIFE on the Bhutani nomogram, apply the 2022 AAP threshold for a 36-week infant with neurotoxicity risk factors, and initiate phototherapy
opts.rat: Hour-of-life-based risk stratification drives the action. RN role: risk-stratify and act.

opts.text: Place opaque eye shields throughout phototherapy and verify they are intact and correctly positioned; remove only briefly during structured parent-feeding interaction
opts.rat: Prevents retinal damage while preserving parent-infant time. RN role: eye protection.

opts.text: Maximize skin exposure (diaper only or naked in a controlled thermal environment), reposition every 2-3 hours, maintain correct lamp distance, and use a fiber-optic 'bili blanket' as an adjunct
opts.rat: Maximizes phototherapy effectiveness. RN role: technique.

opts.text: Monitor axillary temperature every 1-2 hours; use an isolette/radiant warmer to keep thermal balance stable
opts.rat: Both hyperthermia and hypothermia are risks under lights. RN role: temperature.

opts.text: Provide hydration through INCREASED breastfeeding every 2-3 hours (8-12 feedings/24 hours) with lactation support and supplement with expressed breast milk or formula per provider if intake is inadequate
opts.rat: Optimized feeding clears bilirubin via stool/urine and addresses breastfeeding jaundice. RN role: feeding + lactation.

opts.text: Track wet and stool diapers, daily weight, TSB on the provider-ordered schedule, and surveillance for acute bilirubin encephalopathy
opts.rat: Trend monitoring + BIND vigilance. RN role: monitor.

opts.text: Teach the parents the cephalocaudal jaundice check, 8-12 feeds/24h with diaper counts, warning signs, and coordinate a rebound TSB and primary care follow-up within 24-48 hours of discharge
opts.rat: Closes the loop into the home and prevents readmission. RN role: teaching + follow-up.

opts.text: Give plain water or dextrose-water between feedings to flush the bilirubin
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG — worsens jaundice and risks hyponatremia. Treat with optimized breastfeeding.

opts.text: Cover the infant with a blanket under the phototherapy lights to keep warm and apply baby lotion to keep the skin moisturized
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG — blankets block light and lotions can burn under phototherapy.

opts.text: Use phototherapy on a newborn with elevated CONJUGATED (direct) bilirubin to clear the jaundice
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG — phototherapy is for unconjugated hyperbilirubinemia; in conjugated hyperbilirubinemia it risks 'bronze baby' syndrome.

steps.title: Take Action
steps.inst: Rank the order of the nurse's actions. #1 = FIRST.

opts.text: Risk-stratify by HOUR OF LIFE on the Bhutani nomogram with AAP thresholds and initiate phototherapy if indicated
opts.rat: #1 — FIRST. The decision to treat is hour-of-life-based and risk-adjusted. RN role: risk-stratify and act.

opts.text: Apply opaque eye shields and expose maximum skin (diaper only), reposition every 2-3 hours, maintain correct lamp distance, and use a fiber-optic blanket as an adjunct
opts.rat: #2 — Second. Safe technique drives effective phototherapy. RN role: technique.

opts.text: Maintain temperature stability and hydrate through INCREASED breastfeeding (8-12 feedings/24h) with lactation support; no water or dextrose-water
opts.rat: #3 — Third. Thermal balance and feeding-based hydration sustain safe phototherapy. RN role: temperature + feeding.

opts.text: Monitor TSB on schedule, watch for acute bilirubin encephalopathy each shift, discontinue phototherapy below the AAP threshold, and obtain a rebound TSB 12-24 hours after stopping
opts.rat: #4 — Fourth. Trend monitoring + BIND vigilance close the safety loop. RN role: monitor + vigilance.

opts.text: Teach the parents (cephalocaudal check, feeding goals, warning signs, no water/dextrose) and coordinate outpatient bilirubin recheck and primary care/lactation follow-up
opts.rat: #5 — Closing. Parent teaching + outpatient follow-up prevent readmission and complete the plan. RN role: teaching + follow-up.

steps.title: Evaluate Outcomes
steps.inst: For each finding, classify the trajectory.

opts.text: After 12 hours of intensive phototherapy with optimized breastfeeding and lactation support, TSB is trending down, the infant has more wet diapers and loose green stools, weight is stabilizing, and jaundice is receding cephalocaudally
opts.rat: The expected response to effective phototherapy plus improved feeding. Improving trajectory.

opts.text: TSB falls below the AAP threshold and phototherapy is discontinued; eye shields are removed; a rebound TSB is ordered 12-24 hours after stopping
opts.rat: Appropriate de-escalation with closed-loop rebound monitoring. Correct trajectory.

opts.text: Parents teach back the cephalocaudal jaundice check, feeding goals (8-12/24h), warning signs, and the outpatient bilirubin recheck plan
opts.rat: Verified understanding supports safe transition home. Correct trajectory.

opts.text: At outpatient follow-up the rebound TSB is safe for hour of life, breastfeeding is well-established with lactation support, and weight gain has resumed
opts.rat: Sustained safe trajectory with breastfeeding preserved. Ideal outcome.

opts.text: Alternative scenario — the parents are told to give water and dextrose-water between feedings; the infant becomes more dehydrated, the TSB rises, and a high-pitched cry develops
opts.rat: ⚠️ Water/dextrose-water worsen jaundice and risk hyponatremia; the high-pitched cry signals early bilirubin encephalopathy. Demonstrates the rule against water/dextrose.

opts.text: Alternative scenario — the infant is wrapped in a blanket under the lights with baby lotion applied; phototherapy is ineffective and a skin burn develops
opts.rat: ⚠️ Blankets block the light and lotions burn. Demonstrates correct technique — diaper only, no skin products.

opts.text: Alternative scenario — eye shields are repeatedly left off because they keep falling; retinal damage develops
opts.rat: ⚠️ Eye shields are required throughout phototherapy. Demonstrates the eye-protection rule.

opts.text: Alternative scenario — breastfeeding is routinely stopped 'because of the jaundice' and the infant is switched to formula for a week against the family's wishes
opts.rat: ⚠️ Routine interruption of breastfeeding is not recommended; optimize breastfeeding with lactation support and supplement with expressed milk/formula only as needed.

opts.text: Alternative scenario — phototherapy is initiated for elevated CONJUGATED (direct) bilirubin; the infant develops 'bronze baby' discoloration
opts.rat: ⚠️ Phototherapy is for unconjugated hyperbilirubinemia only; conjugated hyperbilirubinemia needs a different workup (think biliary atresia, sepsis).

opts.text: In the first hours of phototherapy, before the bilirubin has fully responded, the TSB is essentially unchanged while the infant is feeding more vigorously and the technique is correct
opts.rat: Expected — phototherapy takes hours to drive a measurable TSB fall; continue safe care and trend. Unchanged is appropriate here.

reference: Aligned to the NCSBN NCLEX Test Plan client-need category: Health Promotion and Maintenance, using the NCSBN Clinical Judgment Measurement Model. Written from standard nursing references. Pending faculty review.
